lessons.title: Properties of Gases and Kinetic Theory

content.body: Gases have unique properties explained by the kinetic molecular theory: gas particles are in constant random motion, far apart, with negligible intermolecular forces.

Kinetic Molecular Theory of Gases:
• Gas particles are tiny, widely spaced (mostly empty space)
• Particles in constant random motion
• Elastic collisions (no energy loss overall)
• No intermolecular forces between particles
• Average kinetic energy ∝ absolute temperature (Kelvin)

content.title: Gas Properties
content.body:
Pressure: force per unit area due to particle collisions with walls
Volume: gas fills and takes shape of container
Temperature: measure of average kinetic energy
Density: low (particles widely spaced)

Diffusion: gases spread from high to low concentration
Rate ∝ 1/√(molar mass) — lighter gases diffuse faster

Converting temperature:
K = °C + 273
0°C = 273 K (STP)
STP: 0°C (273K), 1 atm pressure
RTP: 25°C (298K), 1 atm pressure
Molar volume at STP = 22.4 L/mol
Molar volume at RTP = 24.0 L/mol

questions.question: According to kinetic theory, gas pressure is caused by
questions.explanation: Gas pressure arises from the force of gas particles bombarding the walls of the container.

questions.question: As temperature increases, gas particles
options: have greater average kinetic energy and move faster
questions.answer: have greater average kinetic energy and move faster
questions.explanation: Temperature is proportional to average kinetic energy. Higher T = faster moving particles.

questions.question: Lighter gases diffuse faster because
options: they have higher velocity at same temperature (KE=½mv²)
questions.answer: they have higher velocity at same temperature (KE=½mv²)
questions.explanation: At same temperature, all gases have same average KE. Lighter molecules (smaller m) must have higher velocity v.

questions.question: Molar volume at STP (0°C, 1 atm) is
questions.explanation: At STP, one mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 dm³ (litres).

questions.question: Temperature in Kelvin = temperature in °C plus
questions.explanation: K = °C + 273. Absolute zero (0 K) = -273°C — the lowest possible temperature.

questions.question: Elastic collisions between gas particles mean
options: kinetic energy is conserved — no net energy loss in collisions
questions.answer: kinetic energy is conserved — no net energy loss in collisions
questions.explanation: Elastic collisions conserve total kinetic energy — gas maintains its energy at constant temperature.

questions.question: Gas diffusion is the
options: movement of gas from high to low concentration
questions.answer: movement of gas from high to low concentration
questions.explanation: Diffusion: net movement of particles down a concentration gradient from high to low concentration.

questions.question: At RTP (room temperature and pressure), the molar volume of a gas is
questions.explanation: At RTP (25°C, 1 atm), the molar volume of an ideal gas is approximately 24.0 dm³/mol.

questions.question: Gas molecules have negligible intermolecular forces. This explains why gases
options: are easily compressed and expand to fill containers
questions.answer: are easily compressed and expand to fill containers
questions.explanation: Without attractive forces holding particles together, gas molecules move freely in all directions, filling any container.

questions.question: If a gas has mass 44g/mol and another has mass 4g/mol, which diffuses faster?
questions.explanation: Graham's law: lighter gas (smaller molar mass) diffuses faster. 4g/mol (like He) diffuses much faster than 44g/mol (CO₂).

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: The gas laws describe how pressure, volume, and temperature of a gas are related. They apply to ideal gases where intermolecular forces are negligible.

Boyle's Law (constant T):
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂
Pressure and volume are inversely proportional

Charles' Law (constant P):
V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂
Volume and absolute temperature are directly proportional

Pressure Law (constant V):
P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂

Combined/Ideal Gas Law:
PV/T = constant
P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂
PV = nRT (n=moles, R=8.314 J/mol/K)

content.title: Gas Law Calculations
content.body:
Boyle's: P=100kPa, V=3L → V at P=150kPa?
100×3 = 150×V₂ → V₂ = 2L

Charles': V=4L at 300K → V at 450K?
4/300 = V₂/450 → V₂ = 6L

Ideal Gas: find moles of gas if P=101325 Pa, V=24L, T=293K
n = PV/RT = 101325×0.024/(8.314×293) = 1.0 mol

content.title: Real vs Ideal Gases
content.body:
Ideal gas: follows PV=nRT exactly (no intermolecular forces, particles have zero volume)
Real gases deviate at:
• High pressure (particles closer, forces matter)
• Low temperature (particles slower, forces more significant)
Noble gases closest to ideal

questions.question: Boyle's Law states (constant temperature)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: pressure and volume are inversely proportional at constant temperature.

questions.question: Gas at 200 kPa, 5L. New volume at 400 kPa (same T)?
Charles' Law states (constant pressure)
questions.explanation: Charles' Law: volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature (Kelvin) at constant pressure.

questions.question: Gas at 300K occupies 6L. Volume at 600K (same P)?
questions.explanation: V₁/T₁=V₂/T₂: 6/300=V₂/600, V₂=12L

questions.question: In PV=nRT, R is
questions.explanation: R is the universal gas constant = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.

questions.question: Temperature must be in Kelvin for gas laws because
options: Celsius has negative values that would give meaningless results
questions.answer: Celsius has negative values that would give meaningless results
questions.explanation: Negative Celsius temperatures would give negative volumes/pressures in equations — physically impossible. Kelvin starts at absolute zero.

questions.question: Real gases deviate most from ideal at
questions.explanation: At high P: particles close together (volume matters). Low T: slow particles (intermolecular forces become significant).

questions.question: How many moles in 44.8L of gas at STP?
questions.explanation: At STP, 22.4L = 1 mol. So 44.8L = 2 mol.

questions.question: Which gas behaves most like an ideal gas?
questions.explanation: Noble gases (especially He — smallest, no polarity) have minimal intermolecular forces, closest to ideal gas behaviour.

questions.question: If pressure doubles and temperature doubles (Kelvin), volume
questions.explanation: PV=nRT: if both P and T double, V stays same (PV/T = constant: 2P×V/2T = PV/T).

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: A solution consists of a solute dissolved in a solvent. The most common solvent is water. Concentration measures how much solute is dissolved in a given volume.

Concentration:
c = n/V (mol/dm³ or mol/L)
n = number of moles = mass/molar mass
Mass concentration (g/dm³) = c × molar mass

Dilution:
c₁V₁ = c₂V₂ (moles of solute constant)

Solubility:
Mass of solute in 100g water at given temperature
Affected by temperature (most solids: ↑T → ↑solubility)
Gases: ↑T → ↓solubility; ↑P → ↑solubility

content.title: Concentration Calculations
content.body:
Find concentration of 4g NaOH (40g/mol) in 500cm³:
n = 4/40 = 0.1 mol
c = 0.1/0.5 = 0.2 mol/dm³

Dilution: 25cm³ of 2M HCl to 500cm³:
c₂ = c₁V₁/V₂ = 2×25/500 = 0.1 M

content.title: Solubility Curves
content.body:
Solubility curves show how solubility changes with temperature.
At a given temperature, a saturated solution contains maximum dissolved solute.
If a hot saturated solution cools, excess solute crystallises out.
This is used to purify solids (recrystallisation).

questions.question: Concentration in mol/dm³ =
questions.explanation: Concentration c = n/V where n is moles of solute and V is volume in dm³.

questions.question: Moles of 5g of NaCl (molar mass 58.5 g/mol)
questions.explanation: n = mass/molar mass = 5/58.5 = 0.085 mol

questions.question: Concentration of 0.2 mol in 250cm³ (0.25dm³)
Dilution formula c₁V₁ = c₂V₂ works because
options: number of moles of solute stays the same
questions.answer: number of moles of solute stays the same
questions.explanation: Adding water increases volume but doesn't add/remove solute — moles stay constant, concentration decreases.

questions.question: 25cm³ of 3M solution diluted to 75cm³. New concentration =
questions.explanation: c₂ = c₁V₁/V₂ = 3×25/75 = 1M

questions.question: Most solid solutes become more soluble when temperature increases because
options: increased molecular motion overcomes lattice energy
questions.answer: increased molecular motion overcomes lattice energy
questions.explanation: Higher temperature provides more energy to break the ionic/molecular lattice, allowing more solute to dissolve.

questions.question: Gas solubility in water increases when
questions.explanation: Henry's Law: gas solubility ∝ partial pressure of gas above solution. Temperature increase actually decreases gas solubility.

questions.question: A saturated solution is one that
options: cannot dissolve any more solute at that temperature
questions.answer: cannot dissolve any more solute at that temperature
questions.explanation: A saturated solution is at equilibrium — maximum solute is dissolved; adding more leaves undissolved solid.

questions.question: Recrystallisation purifies solids by
options: dissolving in hot water, filtering impurities, cooling to crystallise pure solid
questions.answer: dissolving in hot water, filtering impurities, cooling to crystallise pure solid
questions.explanation: Hot solution dissolves impure solid. Filter off insoluble impurities. Cool — pure compound crystallises out, leaving soluble impurities in solution.

questions.question: Standard solution is one with
questions.explanation: A standard solution has an accurately known concentration, used as a reference in titrations.

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Gases Solutions. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Gases Solutions concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Gases Solutions builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Gases Solutions. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Gases Solutions. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Gases Solutions include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Gases Solutions. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
